topic: AI Agent Observability with OpenTelemetry: From Dev to Production in 2026

key_points: Covers full OTel + VictoriaMetrics stack setup for AI agent telemetry
Demonstrates traces, logs, and metrics pipeline from agent to backend
Shows self-hosted approach with Prometheus-compatible metrics storage
Focuses on token usage and latency metrics with Grafana dashboards
Comprehensive LLM and agent observability guide using Uptrace as backend
Explains GenAI semantic conventions with concrete span attribute examples
Covers auto-instrumentation for OpenAI, Anthropic, LangChain, LlamaIndex
Shows how nested spans work for agent reasoning chains and tool calls
Broad overview covering tracing, evaluation, and prompt management
Emphasizes AI-native tools vs traditional APM for agent debugging
Covers session-level vs request-level observability distinctions
Discusses cost tracking as integral part of observability strategy
Official OTel blog covering GenAI observability features in 2026
Explains span events vs span attributes for prompt/completion content
Covers opt-in content capture to avoid storing sensitive PII by default
Shows gen_ai.client.token.usage and gen_ai.client.operation.duration metrics
Deep dive into GenAI Semantic Conventions SIG progress through Q1 2026
Covers agent-specific spans: gen_ai.agent.name, gen_ai.tool.name attributes
Explains experimental vs stable status of different convention areas
Provides framework-by-framework instrumentation coverage matrix

stats.fact: LLM observability platform market grew to an estimated $2.69 billion in 2026, projected to reach $9.26 billion by 2030 at a 36.2% CAGR
Gartner predicts that by 2028, LLM observability investments will account for 50% of GenAI deployments, up from 15% in early 2026
85% of organizations with GenAI deployments plan for LLM observability as a key infrastructure requirement in 2026
OTel in production nearly doubled year-over-year from 6% to 11% among enterprises; 89% rate vendor compliance with GenAI conventions as critical
Langfuse open-source LLM observability platform surpassed 21,000 GitHub stars by early 2026
Agent execution durations range from 500ms to 3+ minutes; without tracing, identifying which tool call caused a timeout is nearly impossible
OpenTelemetry GenAI client spans and metrics exited experimental status in early 2026; agent and framework spans remain experimental but production-stable

angles: Why traditional APM fails for AI agents: non-deterministic execution paths, token-based cost model, and multi-step reasoning chains require agent-native spans
Zero-to-production OTel setup walkthrough: pip install to auto-instrument to local Jaeger to production Grafana Cloud, showing real span output at each stage
The 6 metrics every production AI agent must track: token usage per run, tool call success rate, LLM latency distribution, agent loop iterations, context window utilization, and end-to-end latency
Content capture safety: GenAI conventions store prompt/completion in span events not attributes by default, enabling GDPR-safe observability with opt-in content tracing
Multi-agent distributed tracing: propagating trace context across agent boundaries so a single trace ID spans coordinator to subagent to tool chains
Cost observability: using gen_ai.client.token.usage spans to build per-agent, per-session, and per-feature cost dashboards without a separate billing proxy
outline: H2: Why Traditional Observability Fails for AI Agents
H2: OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions: The 2026 Standard
H2: Setting Up OpenTelemetry for AI Agents in Python (Step-by-Step)
H2: The 6 Essential Metrics Every Production AI Agent Needs
H2: Distributed Tracing for Multi-Agent and Tool-Calling Workflows
H2: Choosing Your Observability Backend (Self-Hosted vs Managed)
H2: Production Deployment Checklist: Dev to Prod in One Guide
